Title: Design an Onboarding Flow That Reaches the Aha Moment Fast
Teaser: Maps your product's first-run experience to the one action that predicts retention, then designs the shortest path a new user can take to get there.
PromptHint: Describe your product and what a new user must do to first feel value in [PRODUCT]. Add your signup-to-value friction points in [FRICTION] if known.
Prompt:
You are a product-led growth lead who has shipped onboarding for multiple SaaS products and obsesses over time-to-value. Here is the product:
<product>
[PRODUCT]
</product>
Known friction or drop-off points (optional): [FRICTION]
Primary new-user persona (optional): [PERSONA]
Think first, silently: identify the single 'aha moment' — the first action where the user genuinely experiences the core value — and work backwards to the minimum steps required to reach it.
Then design the flow: (1) State the aha moment in one sentence and why it predicts retention. (2) Map the current implied path from signup to aha as numbered steps, flagging every point where a user could stall, get confused, or be asked for something prematurely. (3) Propose a redesigned shortest path, removing or deferring every step not strictly required to reach value. (4) For each remaining step, specify the goal of the screen, the single primary action, and what to do if the user hesitates (empty state, sample data, or skip option). (5) Define one activation metric and the checkpoint where you'd measure it.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not invent benchmark percentages or cite studies. Defer account setup, team invites, and integrations until after the aha moment unless they are the value itself. No dark patterns or forced steps.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Aha moment' line, a Current-path table (Step | Goal | Stall risk), a Redesigned-path table (Step | Screen goal | Primary action | Hesitation handling), then an 'Activation metric & checkpoint' line.

Title: Diagnose Why New Users Aren't Activating
Teaser: Turns your funnel drop-off data into a ranked set of root-cause hypotheses and the cheapest experiment to test each one.
PromptHint: Paste your signup-to-activation funnel numbers and any user feedback into [FUNNEL]. Define what 'activated' means for your product in [ACTIVATION].
Prompt:
You are a growth analyst who diagnoses activation problems without jumping to solutions. Funnel data and any qualitative signals:
<funnel>
[FUNNEL]
</funnel>
Your definition of an 'activated' user: [ACTIVATION]
Product context (optional): [PRODUCT]
Think first, silently: find the single biggest drop-off step in the funnel, then brainstorm whether the cause is motivation (didn't want to), ability (couldn't figure out how), or trigger (nothing prompted them) before ranking.
Then diagnose: (1) Identify the largest leak in the funnel with the actual numbers, and quantify the upside of fixing it. (2) List 4-6 distinct root-cause hypotheses for that leak, each tagged as a Motivation, Ability, or Trigger problem, ordered by how likely and how cheap-to-fix they are. (3) For each hypothesis, name the evidence that would confirm or kill it, and the single cheapest experiment to run (a copy change, a session replay review, a survey question, a removed step). (4) Recommend the one experiment to run first and what result would make you act. (5) Flag any place where the data is too thin to conclude and what to instrument.
CONSTRAINTS: Treat my numbers as the only data; do not invent industry averages. Never recommend a multi-week build before a one-day test. Distinguish correlation from cause explicitly.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Biggest leak' line with numbers, a Hypotheses table (Hypothesis | Type | Likelihood | Test | What would confirm it), then a 'Run this first' recommendation and an 'Instrument this' list.

Title: Write a Churn-Save Sequence for Cancelling Customers
Teaser: Builds a respectful, segmented save flow that surfaces the real reason a customer is leaving and offers a fitting alternative to cancelling outright.
PromptHint: Describe your product, plan, and the typical cancellation reasons in [CONTEXT]. Add the save levers you can actually offer (pause, downgrade, discount) in [LEVERS].
Prompt:
You are a retention specialist who designs cancellation flows that respect the customer and never trap them. Context:
<context>
[CONTEXT]
</context>
Save levers you can offer (e.g., pause, downgrade, extension, discount, white-glove help): [LEVERS]
Brand voice (optional): [VOICE]
Think first, silently: group cancellation reasons into a few buckets (too expensive, not using it, missing a feature, switched tools, temporary need) and match each bucket to the save lever that genuinely fits — not a blanket discount.
Then build the flow: (1) Write the cancellation-reason picker: 4-6 honest options plus an 'other' free-text prompt, worded without guilt. (2) For each reason, write the branch the customer sees — a one-line acknowledgement, the single most fitting offer or alternative, and a clearly visible 'continue cancelling' path. (3) Write the copy for the final confirmation and the post-cancel message (what happens to their data, how to come back, no hard feelings). (4) Write a short win-back email to send 2-4 weeks later, tied to the reason they gave. (5) Note which reasons you should NOT try to save and why fighting them harms the brand.
CONSTRAINTS: This is not legal or financial advice. Never hide the cancel button, add friction steps, or require a phone call to cancel. Make every offer something a fair company would actually honor. No fake scarcity or guilt-tripping.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Reason picker' block, a per-reason Branch table (Reason | Acknowledgement | Offer | Exit copy), a 'Confirmation & post-cancel' block, a win-back email, and a 'Don't fight these' list.

Title: Plan and Announce a Feature Launch That Drives Adoption
Teaser: Turns a shipped feature into a coordinated launch plan plus ready-to-use announcement copy aimed at the users who'll actually benefit.
PromptHint: Describe the feature, who it's for, and the problem it solves in [FEATURE]. Name your channels (in-app, email, changelog, social) in [CHANNELS].
Prompt:
You are a product marketing manager who launches features so the right users adopt them, not just hear about them. The feature:
<feature>
[FEATURE]
</feature>
Launch channels available: [CHANNELS]
Target segment (optional): [SEGMENT]
Think first, silently: pin down the single user problem this feature removes and the 'before vs after' difference in the user's day, then decide which existing users should hear about it first.
Then plan the launch: (1) Write a one-line positioning statement: for [who], who [problem], this feature [benefit], unlike [the old way]. (2) Define the launch tiers — who gets it first (beta/power users), the broad announcement, and any holdouts — with the reasoning. (3) For each named channel, write the actual copy: in-app announcement (headline + 2 lines + CTA), launch email (subject + preview + body + CTA), changelog entry, and a short social post. Match length and tone to the channel. (4) Define the one adoption metric that tells you the launch worked and the timeframe to judge it. (5) List the top objection or confusion this feature might trigger and the FAQ line that defuses it.
CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the user benefit, not the mechanism. No invented metrics, awards, or 'most requested' claims unless I provided them. Keep every CTA to a single clear action.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Positioning' line, a 'Launch tiers' list, per-channel copy blocks clearly labeled, an 'Adoption metric' line, and a 'Top objection + FAQ answer'.

Title: Write Changelog Entries Users Actually Want to Read
Teaser: Converts a dry list of shipped changes into a clear, benefit-led changelog that tells users what's new, why it matters, and what to do next.
PromptHint: Paste your raw release notes, commit summaries, or ticket titles into [CHANGES]. Add your product name and audience in [PRODUCT].
Prompt:
You are a product writer who turns engineering changelogs into something users open by choice. Raw changes from this release:
<changes>
[CHANGES]
</changes>
Product and audience: [PRODUCT]
Tone (optional, e.g., plain / playful / technical): [TONE]
Think first, silently: sort each change into New, Improved, or Fixed; drop internal refactors users can't feel; and for each surviving item identify the user benefit, not the implementation.
Then write the changelog: (1) A short, human intro line summarizing the theme of the release (skip if there's no theme). (2) Three grouped sections — New, Improved, Fixed — each item written as a benefit-led line: what changed and why the user cares, in one or two sentences, with the feature name bolded. (3) For any user-facing behavior change, add a 'what you need to do' note (or 'nothing — it just works'). (4) Call out one headline item to feature at the top if the release has a standout. (5) End with a single line inviting feedback or pointing to docs.
CONSTRAINTS: No internal jargon, ticket numbers, or version-bump noise. Don't list purely internal changes. Don't overhype small fixes. Keep each entry to two sentences max.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Optional intro line, then 'New / Improved / Fixed' sections as bulleted lists with bolded feature names, an optional 'Highlight' callout at top, and a closing feedback line.

Title: Rewrite a Pricing Page That Converts and Clarifies
Teaser: Audits your pricing page for confusion and rebuilds the tier structure, naming, and copy so the right plan is obvious to each buyer.
PromptHint: Describe your current plans, prices, and what each includes in [PRICING]. Name your main customer segments (e.g., solo, team, enterprise) in [SEGMENTS].
Prompt:
You are a pricing-page strategist who designs tiers so each buyer instantly knows which plan is theirs. Current pricing:
<pricing>
[PRICING]
</pricing>
Customer segments and who you most want on each plan: [SEGMENTS]
Primary conversion goal (e.g., trials, paid signups, sales calls) (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: figure out who each tier is FOR (not just what it costs), find the value metric that should scale price, and spot where the current page makes the buyer think too hard or feel nickel-and-dimed.
Then rebuild: (1) Audit the current structure — list the top 3 sources of confusion or friction (too many tiers, unclear differences, hidden value metric, weak plan names). (2) Recommend a tier structure: for each plan, a buyer-focused name, the one-line 'this is for you if...' qualifier, the headline value, and the 3-5 features that actually differentiate it (not a 30-row checklist). (3) Choose the recommended/'most popular' plan and justify the anchor. (4) Write the price-justifying copy and the CTA for each tier. (5) Draft 3 pricing-page FAQ entries that remove the most common purchase hesitations (billing, switching, limits).
CONSTRAINTS: This is informational, not financial advice. Don't invent specific dollar amounts unless I gave them — work with placeholders if needed. Avoid feature-bloat comparison tables; differentiate on outcomes. No fake urgency.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Audit' list (top 3 issues), a Tier table (Plan | For you if | Headline value | Key differentiators | CTA), a 'Recommended plan + why' line, and 3 FAQ entries.

Title: Build a Self-Reinforcing PLG Growth Loop
Teaser: Designs a product-led growth loop where using the product creates the next user, then pressure-tests where the loop leaks.
PromptHint: Describe your product and how a user could naturally expose it to others in [PRODUCT]. Note your current acquisition channels in [CHANNELS] if relevant.
Prompt:
You are a growth architect who designs product-led loops rather than relying on paid acquisition. The product:
<product>
[PRODUCT]
</product>
Current acquisition channels (optional): [CHANNELS]
Core action users take repeatedly: [CORE_ACTION]
Think first, silently: identify whether the natural loop is viral (user invites user), content (user creates artifacts others find), or usage-based (more use creates more value or exposure), then trace the full cycle from new user back to new user.
Then design the loop: (1) State the loop type and write it as a cycle: new user does X, which produces Y, which reaches a new person, who signs up and does X. (2) Identify the single output of normal product use that could expose the product to non-users (a shared link, a public artifact, a 'made with' mark, an invite) and how to make sharing the default, not a chore. (3) For each step of the loop, estimate the conversion bottleneck — where most potential is lost — and the cheapest lever to widen it. (4) Add the incentive design: what the sharer gets and what the new user gets, kept mutual and non-spammy. (5) Define the one loop metric to track (e.g., new users per active user per period) and what 'healthy' looks like directionally.
CONSTRAINTS: No spam mechanics, forced sharing, or contact-list scraping. The loop must create real value for both sides. Don't fabricate viral coefficients; reason directionally. No growth-hacking dark patterns.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Loop type' line, the loop written as a numbered cycle, a Bottleneck table (Step | Where it leaks | Lever), an 'Incentive design' block, and a 'Loop metric to watch' line.

Title: Write In-App Copy That Guides Without Getting in the Way
Teaser: Produces clear, on-brand microcopy for empty states, tooltips, errors, and confirmations that reduces confusion and support tickets.
PromptHint: List the screens or moments needing copy (empty states, errors, tooltips, modals) in [SCREENS]. Describe your product and voice in [PRODUCT].
Prompt:
You are a UX writer who makes interfaces feel effortless through precise microcopy. Screens and moments that need copy:
<screens>
[SCREENS]
</screens>
Product and brand voice: [PRODUCT]
Reading level / tone constraints (optional): [TONE]
Think first, silently: for each moment decide what the user is feeling (lost, blocked, about to do something risky) and what the copy must accomplish — orient, unblock, reassure, or confirm — before writing a word.
Then write the copy: (1) For each screen or moment, provide: a heading (if needed), the body line(s), and the button/CTA label. (2) Empty states: explain what goes here, why it's empty, and the single next action — turn the void into a starting point. (3) Error messages: say what happened in plain language, never blame the user, and give the exact next step to recover. (4) Confirmations for destructive actions: state the consequence clearly and label buttons with verbs, not 'OK/Cancel'. (5) Tooltips/inline help: one sentence, only where genuinely useful. For each item, add a one-line note on the writing intent.
CONSTRAINTS: No jargon, no 'Oops!', no exclamation overload, no blaming language ('you entered an invalid...'). Keep buttons to action verbs. Don't add copy where silence is clearer. Match the stated voice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: One block per screen/moment, each with 'Heading / Body / CTA' fields and a short 'Intent' note. Group by type (Empty states, Errors, Confirmations, Tooltips).

Title: Analyze NPS Responses Into a Prioritized Action List
Teaser: Turns raw NPS scores and verbatim comments into themed drivers, segment differences, and the few fixes that would move the score most.
PromptHint: Paste your NPS scores and open-text comments into [RESPONSES]. Add any segment tags (plan, tenure, role) you have in [SEGMENTS].
Prompt:
You are a customer insights analyst who turns NPS surveys into a clear plan, not a vanity number. Survey data:
<responses>
[RESPONSES]
</responses>
Segment tags available (optional): [SEGMENTS]
Product context (optional): [PRODUCT]
Think first, silently: separate promoters, passives, and detractors, then read the verbatims to find recurring themes and, crucially, what promoters love versus what detractors are blocked by — these usually differ.
Then analyze: (1) Report the overall NPS (and the promoter/passive/detractor split) from the data given. (2) Surface 4-6 recurring themes across the comments, each tagged as a strength (driving promoters), a pain (driving detractors), or a passive-tipping-point, with a representative quote and rough frequency. (3) If segment tags exist, call out where a segment scores notably differently and why. (4) Identify the 'silent detractor' risk — themes mentioned even by some promoters that could erode loyalty. (5) Recommend the top 3 actions ranked by likely score impact versus effort, each tied to the theme it addresses and the segment it serves.
CONSTRAINTS: Quote only from the responses I provided; never invent comments or scores. If a theme is thin (few mentions), say so rather than overstating it. Distinguish what people say from what they do. This is analysis, not a guarantee of score movement.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'NPS & split' line, a Themes table (Theme | Type | Frequency | Representative quote), a 'Segment differences' note, a 'Silent detractor watch' line, and a ranked 'Top 3 actions' list (Action | Theme addressed | Impact | Effort).

Title: Re-Engage Dormant Users Before They Become Churn
Teaser: Segments users who've gone quiet by likely cause and designs a tailored win-back sequence to pull each group back to the core value.
PromptHint: Describe your product, the core value, and what 'dormant' means for you in [CONTEXT]. Add any usage signals you can segment on in [SIGNALS].
Prompt:
You are a lifecycle marketer who revives dormant accounts by addressing why they went quiet, not by nagging. Context:
<context>
[CONTEXT]
</context>
Usage signals you can segment on (last login, features used, setup completed, seats active) (optional): [SIGNALS]
Channels available (email, in-app, push) (optional): [CHANNELS]
Think first, silently: split dormant users into meaningful segments — never activated, activated-then-faded, seasonal/episodic use, blocked by a missing piece — because each needs a different message and a different proof of value.
Then design re-engagement: (1) Define 3-4 dormancy segments with the signal that identifies each and the most likely reason they stopped. (2) For each segment, design a short sequence (2-3 touches) with the goal of each touch, the single message, the proof of value to show (a result they'd get, a feature they missed, their own saved work), and the one re-entry action. (3) Write the copy for the first touch of each segment (subject/headline + body + CTA). (4) Specify the trigger timing and the exit condition (stop messaging once they return or clearly opt out). (5) Recommend which segment to start with for the best effort-to-recovery payoff.
CONSTRAINTS: No guilt, fake-personal 'we miss you' filler, or daily pestering. Cap the sequence and honor opt-outs. Each touch must offer a reason to return, not just a reminder that they left. No invented stats.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A Segments table (Segment | Identifying signal | Likely reason | Re-entry action), per-segment sequence outlines, the first-touch copy for each segment, a 'Timing & exit rules' block, and a 'Start here' recommendation.

Title: Pressure-Test a Feature Idea Before You Build It
Teaser: Runs a feature request through demand, fit, effort, and risk checks so you build what matters and kill what doesn't — with the reasoning written down.
PromptHint: Describe the feature being considered and who asked for it in [FEATURE]. Add your product's strategy or focus in [STRATEGY] if you have one.
Prompt:
You are a principal product manager who is the loyal opposition to every feature idea, including your own. The proposal:
<feature>
[FEATURE]
</feature>
Product strategy or current focus (optional): [STRATEGY]
Who is asking and how often (optional): [DEMAND]
Think first, silently: separate the requested solution from the underlying job-to-be-done, and ask whether this is a real, recurring problem for a segment you care about — or a loud minority's wish.
Then pressure-test: (1) Restate the actual user problem behind the request (the job-to-be-done), separate from the proposed solution. (2) Demand check: who has this problem, how often, and how painful is it without a fix — is this a vitamin or a painkiller? (3) Fit check: does this advance the strategy, or pull focus toward a different product? (4) Alternatives: list 2-3 cheaper or simpler ways to solve the same job, including 'configure existing features' or 'do nothing'. (5) Risk check: what does this add to support, complexity, and roadmap that you'll carry forever? (6) Give a clear verdict — Build now / Build later / Solve differently / Decline — with the one piece of evidence that would change your mind.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not rubber-stamp; argue the case against building. No invented user counts or revenue figures. Treat 'a big customer asked' as a data point, not a decision. Be specific about the cheaper alternative.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Real problem' restatement, a 'Demand / Fit / Risk' assessment block, an 'Alternatives' list, a bolded 'Verdict' with reasoning, and a 'What would change my mind' line.

Title: Map Free-to-Paid Conversion Triggers for a Freemium Plan
Teaser: Finds the natural moments where a free user hits the value of upgrading and designs honest in-product nudges that convert without resentment.
PromptHint: Describe your free and paid tiers and what each unlocks in [PLANS]. Note what free users typically do before they'd need to upgrade in [BEHAVIOR].
Prompt:
You are a monetization strategist who converts free users at the moment the paid value becomes obvious — never before. Plans:
<plans>
[PLANS]
</plans>
Typical free-user behavior and where they hit limits: [BEHAVIOR]
Upgrade goal (optional, e.g., trial-to-paid, free-to-paid): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: distinguish the free tier's job (let users reach the aha moment and build a habit) from the paywall's job (capture value when usage outgrows free), and find the 'natural upgrade moments' where a user genuinely wants more — not arbitrary caps.
Then map the conversion: (1) Confirm what the free tier must let users accomplish so they form a habit worth paying to keep. (2) Identify 3-5 natural upgrade triggers — moments where the user hits a real limit, wants a power feature, or scales up — and rate each on how strong the intent to pay is. (3) For each trigger, design the in-context nudge: where it appears, the value-framed message (what they unlock, not what they're denied), and the path to upgrade. (4) Spot any 'resentment risk' — limits that feel punitive or block the core value — and recommend fixing the limit instead of monetizing it. (5) Recommend the single highest-intent trigger to optimize first and the metric to watch.
CONSTRAINTS: This is informational, not financial advice. Never gate the core aha moment behind the paywall or use bait-and-switch limits. Frame upgrades as unlocking value, not removing it. No fake countdown timers or invented conversion stats.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Free tier job' line, a Triggers table (Trigger | Intent strength | Nudge placement | Value-framed message), a 'Resentment risks to fix' list, and a 'Optimize this first + metric' recommendation.
